my position is that you should maximize doing good stuff the natural way
then
test and see what you are missing
then
keep doing good stuff, add modifications for your shortcomings and also add something that you know that you are missing.

Live according to good Circadian Rhythm
be naked (without anything sunscreens, moisturizers or eye cover), and be active under the sun.
do not use artificial lights indoor, red is ok, sleep in totally dark room.
do not change hours, ie. do not travel over time zones

### taiyang — 2018-01-15T14:59:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-recent-results-need-some-guidance.20707/post-232601>

i will only comment on what i have researched. i'm not a doctor or a nurse. a 97 fasting blood glucose is not officially pre diabetic- you need to be at 100-125 for that (126 is the cut-off set for diabetes/ cut-offs for the US), but 97 is on the high side. i'm low carb, so my triglycerides are in the 30s. my fasting insulin is also 3. so the question in my mind is why do you have what looks like nice low insulin (the same as mine-3) but relatively high triglycerides and fasting morning blood sugar (not out of range, maybe just high for my case)? i'm not saying my following comments apply to you- blood sugar control issues are not just limited to insulin resistance; they can also be related to insulin insufficiency, or not producing enough insulin for the particular load of carbs that is suitable for your heritage. there is a snp (single nucleotide polymorphism) of a gene that in studies has been most associated with the development of type 2 diabetes: TCF7L2. It has a a common polymorphism. i think 33% of europeans have it. further, the mechanism involves insulin insufficiency, not insulin resistance. i have that snp. i can high relatively high blood sugar but low triglycerides and a low fasting insulin. maybe you also have that snp? if you get genetic analysis like 23andme, you will know. my morning fasted blood sugar really improved a lot even with eating more carbs, when i turned off the circuit breaker to my bedroom(if you can't do that, try unplugging everything in your bedroom before you sleep, plus unplug anything in other rooms that share a wall with your bedroom might drop your morning blood sugar). if i were you i would get a glucose meter and strips and start testing. here in the US i can buy them relatively cheaply from Wal mart and i don't have a prescription.

It has a a common polymorphism. i think 33% of europeans have it. further, the mechanism involves insulin insufficiency, not insulin resistance. i have that snp. i can high relatively high blood sugar but low triglycerides and a low fasting insulin. maybe you also have that snp? if you get genetic analysis like 23andme, you will know. my morning fasted blood sugar really improved a lot even with eating more carbs, when i turned off the circuit breaker to my bedroom(if you can't do that, try unplugging everything in your bedroom before you sleep, plus unplug anything in other rooms that share a wall with your bedroom might drop your morning blood sugar). if i were you i would get a glucose meter and strips and start testing. here in the US i can buy them relatively cheaply from Wal mart and i don't have a prescription.

### taiyang — 2018-01-16T13:04:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-recent-results-need-some-guidance.20707/post-232653>

That's super. We have to keep in mind, however, that even the best meters (sold in the US) only are required to be accurate with 15 points. So a 92 could really be 92 + 15= 107 or 92-15= 77. Also, make sure to have good habits when testing. I always wash my hands to make sure I don't have any little bit of food on them, which would make a higher blood sugar reading. Then I wipe away the first drop of blood and test the second drop. If I get a strange reading, I test again. I use a lancer (needle holder) made by accucheck which hurts a lot less, and I prick the sides of my pinkie finger most of the time, so it doesn't really hurt. Lastly, never share your lancer (needle thingy) with others because of the risk of transmitting bloodborne diseases. Did you unplug everything in your bedroom before you went to sleep? Why do you think you got a lower reading?

### Antonis — 2018-01-27T17:31:23+0200

### taiyang — 2018-01-27T18:57:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-recent-results-need-some-guidance.20707/post-233638>

I asked my diabetic group as I think c-peptide is used as a first step to screen for problems with insulin secretion- ie not enough insulin being produced. this is the response i got:

**"Your reading of 157 pmol/L is low.**

I believe this suggests a diagnosis of type 1 diabetes. I am not a doctor and this is a doctor’s call. As [@Tim35](https://forum.tudiabetes.org/u/tim35)said, you will need to discuss this with your doctor."

Tim said to go screen for tests for the antibodies associated with Type 1 diabetes. I would say that in my case i am not type 1 (based on symptoms, but i never had a c peptide or antibodies test done), but seem to have insufficient insulin to handle carb meals that are fine for my husband. so the short term strategy is to do what you are doing and 1) eat healthy low carb. then continue to test meals with your glucose meter- test one to two hours after a meal (some meals like pizza will show spikes in bg hours after words, though). i'm not trying to alarm you. maybe you caught a potential problem early so you should be happy. that's how i feel in my situation anyway.

### Antonis — 2018-01-27T19:54:21+0200

>

>
> Tim said to go screen for tests for the antibodies associated with Type 1 diabetes. I would say that in my case i am not type 1 (based on symptoms, but i never had a c peptide or antibodies test done), but seem to have insufficient insulin to handle carb meals that are fine for my husband. so the short term strategy is to do what you are doing and 1) eat healthy low carb. then continue to test meals with your glucose meter- test one to two hours after a meal (some meals like pizza will show spikes in bg hours after words, though). i'm not trying to alarm you. maybe you caught a potential problem early so you should be happy. that's how i feel in my situation anyway.

I don't know but it may be as you said. I will have a check. My glucose is low tho so I don't know. I don't have any symptoms.

### taiyang — 2018-01-28T15:09:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-recent-results-need-some-guidance.20707/post-233667>

another diabetic friend said: "Marginally low reading with normal fasting glucose level not necessarily relevant, though. Would be a smoking gun if high fasting glucose levels and low c-peptide. Doctor would have to evaluate it in context of her overall history." To him, since your fasting blood glucose was normal and not high, that low c-peptide might be nothing. Still, he says you need a good doctor to look at your overall history. You might just be like me- we need to eat low carb to be healthy, but we aren't in danger of becoming type one. that's how i am living my life anyway. and i still monitor my blood sugar at least every morning. if it starts to trend up, i will monitor more. so i am not always sticking myself with a needle.

### DrStephane — 2018-09-23T00:19:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-recent-results-need-some-guidance.20707/post-251550>

This forum or any online venture is not a place to diagnose or practice medicine, however here is my 2 cents base on Functional Medicine values.

1.  TSH 0.876 mIU/L (0.350 - 4.940) **LOW**

    Free T4 14.61 pmol/L (9.00 - 19.05) **HIGH**

    Free T3 3.67 pmol/L (2.63 - 5.70)

    Prolactin 65.3 mIU/L (72.66 - 407.4)

    Insulin 3.0 μIU/ml (2.0 - 20.0)

    VitaminB12 1236 pg/mL (187 - 883)

    Folate acid 4.20 ng/mL (3.10-20.50)

2.  Glucose 97 mg/dL (74-106)

    Uric Acid 3.5 mg/dL (3.5 - 7.2) **LOW**

    Total Cholesterol 393 mg/dL (100 - 200) **HIGH**

    HDL 78 mg/dL (35 - 65)

    LDL 303 mg/dL (60 - 150) **HIGH**

    Triglycerides 59mg/dL (50 - 150) **LOW**

    Urea 44 mg/dL (17 - 43) **HIGH**

    Creatine 0.70 mg/dL (0.67 - 1.17)

    Magnesium 0.86 mmol/L (0.73 - 1.06) **low**

    Iron 82 μg/dL (70 - 180) **LOW**

    TIBC 294 μg/dL (220 - 460)

    Ferritin 223 ng/mL (20 - 300)

    Total Proteins 7.8 g/dL (6.6 - 8.3) **HIGH**

    Albumin 5.2 g/dL (3.5 - 5.2) **HIGH**

    Globulins 2.6 g/dL (1.4 - 4.8)

    Sodium 139 mmol/L (135 - 145) **LOW**

    Potassium 4.84 mmol/L (3.50 - 5.00)

    Bicarbonate (HCO3) 30.5 mmol/L (21.0 - 31.0)

    Total Bilirubin 0.60 mg/dL (0.30 - 1.20)

    Alkaline Phosphatase 88 U/L 30 - 120 **HIGH**

    γ glutamyltransferase 19 U/L (9 - 55)

    Alkaline Aminotransferase 38 U/L (3 - 41) **HIGH**

    Aspartate Aminotransferase 35 U/L (3 - 38) **HIGH**

    Lactate Dehydrogenase 338 U/L (208 - 480) **HIGH**

    Creatine Kinase 158 U/L (26 - 171)

    IgG 988.00 mg/dL (700.00 - 1600.00)
    IgA 130.00 mg/dL (70.00 - 400.00)
    IgM 93.00 mg/dL (40.00 - 230.00)

Again, without a proper history, timeline and setting these values means nothing!

Questions:
Are you following Dr Kruse protocol? if so, did you prior this lab testing? if so, how long prior? If not, apply Dr. Kruse protocol and retest in 3 months

In case this get asked:
Preliminary recommend further testing:
CBC with differential
Vit D
Breath Test

Hint:
You have alot of Online companies that you can put your blood values and get somewhat a reading. This is a cheaper way to start prior finding a Dr. Kruse near your area.

Dr S

Perhaps I am misunderstanding but it sounded as if Jack told one of the callers to go tanning and if they noticed they felt worse then they knew where an issue lied. What I think I am understanding is if there is something wrong with semiconduction the sun can make you feel sick? Can someone correct me if I am wrong and if I am not can someone help me understand this concept better or direct me to the material?

I'm pretty sure doc has said sunlight is a way to improve redox, 90% sure this is how I improved. My D was low and my immune system seemed to be affected, so my focus was upping my D.
If your cellular water is bad (chronic dehydration) then the three legged stool should be your best friend.
I don't think there's an issue with red light adversely affecting anyone. I see red light as ALWAYS good.

# Thread: Reading an MRI for redox
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reading-an-mri-for-redox.20713/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lucien Burke — 2018-01-17T17:50:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reading-an-mri-for-redox.20713/post-232748>

Does anyone know the specifics on how to read an MRI to learn about your redox potential? I know Dr. Kruse has mentioned it before. I have multiple brain MRI images on my computer from when I was in a car accident. I didn’t get a head injury, but now I realize the images could come in handy to learn about my redox.

I know the basis of MRIs is to get a picture of the light release from excited charged particles when you shoot a magnetic field at them. I don’t really know what to look for when I’m looking at my own images though. Brightness? Dark spots? The more light release the lower the redox?

If anyone knows, give me a heads up

### ObstreperousPolarBear — 2018-01-20T19:34:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reading-an-mri-for-redox.20713/post-232972>

Bump. I would like to know the answer to this as well.

Lucien - even though I don't know what exactly to look for, what I can tell you is that sometimes radiologists (the guys who read MRIs) will include verbiage in their reports that is extremely non-specific. Like "stranding" or "hyperattenuation." I haven't heard Jack mention one of these terms as a marker for inflammation in what I have listened/read of his so far - but I'd really like to. You may find your answer in the report, without having to go back and read the initial image.

I couldn't find a good answer on radiopaedia - maybe someone else can.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-20T20:05:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reading-an-mri-for-redox.20713/post-232978>

There is a Redox Rx two blog on the topic. Search for it.

### drezy — 2018-01-20T20:56:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reading-an-mri-for-redox.20713/post-232986>

Google search protip:

Code:

```
redox mri site:www.jackkruse.com
```

Rinse and repeat for all interests. Jack wrote metric shit tons of material.

### Janet Shold — 2018-01-18T08:22:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-curious.20717/post-232810>

I’m 58, and 10 years post menopause. Since starting cold thermogenesis I have started spotting afterwards. Not every time. This evening I did 35 minutes in a cold tub and have some bleeding. There is a definite corellation between the cold therapy and spotting/bleeding. It’s been years since any of this crap, whatever could be causing it? Are hormones readjusting, do I have some fybroids dissolving? I’m very puzzled. No cramping or anything like that.

### Janet Shold — 2018-09-12T08:40:30+0300

Should I continue with CT? I will definitely continue greeting the sunrise and blocking evening lights. Also continue eating right, making sure natural DNA (sn2) is on the menu.

What else can I do?

### Jack Kruse — 2018-09-12T23:50:59+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2018-09-12T23:52:17+0300

### Janet Shold — 2018-09-13T01:09:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-curious.20717/post-250869>

It's all early. They tell me I'm to have a PET scan and an MRI. Also there is talk of radiation and chemo. I don't know if my immune system or the rest of me can handle that. Damnit!

I am very strict about light use except that I do read at night with a red light. Maybe I'm not strict enough! I have been following a ketogenic diet since last December.

I'll definitely ask about the papaloma virus. I'm surprised they haven't brought it up yet.

I have to add, though, that when I joined I was still quite ill - with lyme symptoms (fatigue, headaches, and so on), but since following the advice here with cold therapy, ketogenic diet, watching sunrise and getting sunlight, I am at least 90% of my former self. # Thread: Greeting from Pittsburgh
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greeting-from-pittsburgh.20721/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gregory H Hoeper — 2018-01-18T13:36:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greeting-from-pittsburgh.20721/post-232819>

Good morning all. My name is Gregory H. Hoeper, and I am a chiropractor from the Northern Pittsburgh area. I am new to practice (just over 8 months), and have been running a functional medicine practice since the day I was licensed and graduated (in 2017). I have had great results with a majority of people (especially in pediatric cases), but it feels as though I am missing something. I use supplements, dietary and lifestyle recommendations, as well as some adjunctive therapies like LLLT, percussive instruments, as well as manual manipulation.

I have been recommending to almost all of my patients a paleolithic ketogenic diet (but didn't emphasize the seafood, which now I do), with compliance taken into account. Some people who come have not gotten to the point where they are ready to give up some foods in the winter (like carbs) and take it to the next level. I have a patient who lives in her house with no sunlight because it makes her sick, and leaves her kids in the evening to sleep in a tent due to surrounding wifi signals away from her children, so that was my first blatant emf sickness (come to find out everything is related to EMFs, magnetism, light, etc). This sn't how I ended up here though.

Anyway, I'm always reading all of the time. Forums, papers, etc trying to get to the bottom of all the issues different people are having, and it has taken me all over the map, so I will tell you how I ended up here.

1) I was visiting my sister (a chiropractor on the other side of Pennsylvania) and she came in at night and looks like she was left in the freezer. Said she drove home with the windows open the whole time for cold exposure. I have done cold exposure, but was introduces by Wim Hoff, so in the shower mostly but occasionally outside. Then she mentioned Jack Kruse and I said "whoever the hell that guy is." Exposure 1.

2) I like to learn about fitness etc., and used to follow someone called Naudi Aguilar. Now when he first started out putting up his original videos (i cam e across him in 2014), they were really great. But over time, he became very toxic, and we actually got in an argument over how the diaphragm works on facebook, and eventually it devolved into some PMs of him being wildly belligerent, aggressive, all those things (I even saved the PMs because he said I was being racist due to Trump winning ?) Anyway, so sometimes I come across his social media videos for a good laugh. One day, I went on and saw a post where he was absolutely disrespecting Dr. Kruse with name-calling and provocative statements (like a teenager). So I checked his last 5 posts on social media, and it was all about Dr. Kruse. But I asked myself a question: "Who the hell is Dr. Jack Kruse, and how did he get this guy so triggered?" Clicked Jack Kruse, say he was a neurosurgeon, say some posts, quantum biology, and said "Another EMFs are bad guy. I know that." Exposure 2.

3) So one day I am browsing "optimising nutrition" facebook group, where people argure about whether 25/50/25 or 23/70/7 or 20/80/0 is the best macronutrient ratio vs. nutrient density vs. some other arbitrary variable is most important in dealing with metabolic/gut dysbiosis/obesity/autoimmune whatever, and people are really getting heated over this. Never seen so many people battle to be the "I'm right" club president ever. And about 20 posts down, I see this comment by Dr. Jack Kruse that says "Obesity begins in the eye, I laid it all out right here in my nourishing vermont 2017 talk." And after that, there was no comments. The group admin asked "what bulbs did you use?" Jack replied, and he said thanks. That was it. The debate over carbohydrates, microflora, insulin, fiber, and protein raged on. And all these red flags went off in my head. I said to myself "Wait a minute, this random ass guy (who I have now heard of on 2 other occasions) pops in and says something radically different than everything all of you are saying, and you just completely ignore it? Wouldn't you at least watch he video, poke holes in everything he says, and continue on?" So I watch the video, and had a panic attack because I realized that everything in my life and practice was going to change. Again." Exposure 3.

So now I am starting to educate my patients on how to reduce EMF exposure, train circadian rhythm, grounding, killing the circuit breaker at night, shutting down wifi, toxic bluelight, and other implementations that I have gathered thus far. I started looking at all of my old patients labs and looked at the values a bit differently, as well as ordering different labs. And again, everything in my life changed. So thanks Dr. Jack Kruse and community for the welcome!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-how-to-help-my-daughter-get-her-head-around-all-this.20723/post-232823>

You are such a knowledgeable group, I feel privileged to be a part of it .
I wondered if anyone has any ideas how I can encourage my daughter (28yrs) to get on board with the self-help discussed on here.
She is bright and slim - so she thinks her constant carbs are not affecting her too much, she is dyslexic and has Irlens syndrome, which make me think she is especially sensitive to blue light and nnEMF. She works on a computer all day. I have at last got her to wear blue blocking glasses a few days ago, and she tells me she is using them at the right times.
Her pupils are massive most of the time, and I have not been able to explain to her the true significance of this - although I know its not good. I noticed her front teeth yesterday are becoming transparent, and know this is another important sign of impending issues.
I have been sharing what I am learning with her since I found you all in September, and some of it is encouraging her to reassess her choices.
Can you think of any key points that would be crucial to share with her to help her thought processes so she take all this more seriously?
My family all work together in a natural food shop in the UK , and we spend all day taking with people about their health issues, trying to shed some light on them, but sometimes those closest to you are the hardest to help...

Oddly the one that was most vehemently (and angrily) convicted it was all bad for us just had a turnaround and said "You all look really good with some color to you even though it's winter. I'm going to try get in the sun more." I hadn't expected that. It was the healthier parent, but I'll take what I can get.

I think it's important to keep being the example while you learn to relate and explain it to others. Good luck.

Many experts on Sun and solar cycles expect our biggest threat from GSM is widespread crop failures and subsequent starvation of world's vulnerable populations. Crops will fail because of shorter growing seasons and erratic weather patterns (destructive cold snaps in spring and fall, massive hail and wind storms, etc.) This situation is expected to reach crisis proportions by 2028.

**Canada**, as one example, is expected to be no longer a food-producing and exporting nation.

Light from sun will be diminished by by lower solar irradiance and by heavy cloud/dust cover in atmosphere-- caused by cosmic rays plus increased volcanic activity. Magnetism is falling on both sun and earth (sun's heliosphere is protective to solar system, and earth magnetism deflects much of solar wind and cosmic radiation).

Earth's geomagnetic field has been dropping rapidly since 1800s and is accelerating in weakness in last few decades. Earth's magnetic poles are on the move, with North Pole marching towards Siberia and South Pole is moving and is now situated completely off Antarctica. # Thread: Was it the oyster, mackerel pate, CT or a bug?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/was-it-the-oyster-mackerel-pate-ct-or-a-bug.20736/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Nigel Clack — 2018-01-21T19:11:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/was-it-the-oyster-mackerel-pate-ct-or-a-bug.20736/post-233073>

A week ago yesterday (Saturday) I had my first raw oyster - pleasantly surprised
On Sunday I finished up some mackerel pate which would of been at the end of it's eat date.
Sunday night I had a infra red sauna and a cold bath, the temp dropped to about 48 degrees, I was only in for about 15 mins. I've only done half a dozen baths and this was the coldest.

Since eating my dinner I felt slightly uncomfortable and bloated.
Around 1 AM I woke and threw up for the next 8 hours or so and had diarrhoea for the next 3 days.

I still have a soft stool and it is pale and thinking about it, it has been since the initial upset. I've never had pale stools before and I have been eating Epi Paleo for about 6 months introducing more fish and shell fish, generally low carb paleo for 6 years.

My wife has had an unsettled stomach for nearly the same time period but no other issues, she didn't have the oyster, pate or a CT session. So I was thinking it was a bug, but why would I have possible bile issues as a consequence.

Are there any enlightened Epi Paleo adapted people out there that can decipher this one please? I have no pain just bloated after meals and I smell

Thanks for any help.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-21T20:35:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/was-it-the-oyster-mackerel-pate-ct-or-a-bug.20736/post-233077>

### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-14T17:06:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-natures-response-to-nnemf-cme.20738/post-237188>

The next 48 hours a G1 class solar flare is heading to Earth. A solar flare/CME typically takes 3 to 5 days to reach the Earth after it leaves the Sun. Observing the ejection of CMEs from the Sun provides an early warning of geomagnetic storms. Only recently, with SOHO, has it been possible to continuously observe the emission of flares/CMEs from the Sun and determine if they are aimed at the Earth. The one ejected today is headed our way. One serious problem that can occur during a geomagnetic storm is damage to Earth-orbiting satellites, especially those in high, geosynchronous orbits. Communications satellites are generally in these high orbits. Either the satellite becomes highly charged during the storm and a component is damaged by the high current that discharges into the satellite, or a component is damaged by high-energy particles that penetrate the satellite. We are not able to predict when and where a satellite in a high orbit may be damaged during a geomagnetic storm. A Chinese satellite is expected to fall from the sky and land in Southern Michigan during this storm. How can this situation teach the mitohacker a deep lesson about the coming 5G world? The heart and cardiovascular system have always been considered the main biological targets of geomagnetic activity. We now know that sleep is affected because of the link of the pineal gland to the superior cervical ganglion and the carotid system that I mentioned in two slides from my Vermont 2017 video. However, the damage inflicted by magnetic storms on human health has been found to be different because people with lowered redox are more at risk. Experts from the Institute of Space Studies of the Russian Academy of Sciences (RAS), Institute of Physics of the Earth (RAS), and the Sechenov Moscow Medical Academy have established that the biggest danger emanates from the micro variations of the geomagnetic field, which coincide with the heartbeat. They occur in about half of all magnetic storms and are most typical for a prelude to a storm with insignificant changes in the geomagnetic field (mostly affecting high altitudes), and for the recovery phase when the geomagnetic field is coming back to normal. So if you love above the 40th latitude in either hemisphere and have heart or brain issues pay attention.

Medical statistics for Moscow show that 70% of all micro variations, caused by geomagnetic disturbances, are accompanied by an abnormally high incidence of heart attacks (a growth of about 13%), and blood-strokes (7.5% growth). The low and extremely low-frequency electromagnetic fields destabilize the heartbeat, leading to a sudden death or infarction. Medical experts have finally explained why heart attacks take a heavy toll before a magnetic storm - because micro variations begin 24 hours before the storm. ER's always report their CCU is more active around solar storms. Tissues with poor mitochondrial function are at risk. Why? CPC #20 tells you why.

"We have analyzed numerous data on the heartbeat of cosmonauts from all Soyuz crews, and the majority of missions to the Mir Station and the International Space Station," said Doctor Tamara Breus (Physics and Mathematics) from the Russian Academy of Sciences' Institute of Space Studies, who heads of a group of physics and physicians, studying this problem.

"We subjected 45 cosmonauts to examination during landing and flights of various duration, when they were exposed to geomagnetic disturbance, and then studied the same parameters in a neutral situation he said.

"The influence of a magnetic storm was obvious in our test subjects. It was manifest in a change of pulse and blood pressure, vegetative disorders, reduction of heart rate variability (HRV) and the power of respiratory undulations, and in a more irregular heartbeat pattern. Rhythm disorders spike. Blood glucose rises. Reactions varied depending on the duration of the flights and an ability of cosmonauts to adapt themselves to the new environment."

In the opinion of Dr. Breus, these effects were a reaction of the vascular tonus and heartbeat rhythm to magnetic storms.

It is perfectly obvious that the response of such a complicated system as the human organism to external factors largely depends on its condition. A magnetic storm is not dangerous for healthy people, but that might not be true for those with poor redox potential.

But there are risk groups with unstable biological status. These include people suffering from ischemic disorders or hypertension, diabetics, or children whose adaptation system is the process of formation. Newly born are, particularly at risk. This new blog is out about how a geomagnetic storm might lead you to make a plan on how to cope with a 5G world. The included video is quite educational. It is called CPC #20: WILL GEOMAGNETIC STORMS MIMIC A 5G WORLD?? [https://www.patreon.com/posts/cpc-20-storms-5g-17538105](https://www.patreon.com/posts/cpc-20-storms-5g-17538105)

# Thread: MRI contrast warning.......
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-contrast-warning.20742/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-22T19:54:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-contrast-warning.20742/post-233174>

**SOURCE:** FDA MedWatch
**ISSUE**: FDA is requiring a new class warning and other safety measures for all gadolinium-based contrast agents (GBCAs) for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) concerning gadolinium remaining in patients' bodies, including the brain, for months to years after receiving these drugs. Gadolinium retention has not been directly linked to adverse health effects in patients with normal kidney function, and FDA has concluded that the benefit of all approved GBCAs continues to outweigh any potential risks. However, after additional review and consultation with the Medical Imaging Drugs Advisory Committee, FDA is requiring several actions to alert health care professionals and patients about gadolinium retention after an MRI using a GBCA, and actions that can help minimize problems. These include requiring a new patient Medication Guide, providing educational information that every patient will be asked to read before receiving a GBCA. FDA is also requiring manufacturers of GBCAs to conduct human and animal studies to further assess the safety of these contrast agents. To date, the only known adverse health effect related to gadolinium retention is a rare condition called nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF) that occurs in a small subgroup of patients with pre-existing kidney failure. FDA received reports of adverse events involving multiple organ systems in patients with normal kidney function. A causal association between these adverse events and gadolinium retention could not be established.

**BACKGROUND**: This is an update to the May 22, 2017 MedWatch safety alert "**[Gadolinium-based Contrast Agents for Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI): Drug Safety Communication - No Harmful Effects Identified With Brain Retention](http://r20.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001j_rjx58yXQeTBx9YFn-xnclTRGEaAqkrUuA52tNVqADHh8IZCT34A8xX7DsmCEVEHrhjJX6DmqKj3sGX9kX1scgZaeTPAnjx3qxIe_ptZW7oRHHxnlpqpWjfudtXYU9itaCJaalgw9htl4Qxo98_GGPvjZaIMShL8izeGXk-sW5vhbZ2fI3OQwMPUUBTEhi0BFEM3xICouHQH28vKZ9UfN4f53KSkyMnCytkt39vunql7giCbZXXmLviIy-r4U6sQ8j8WevYP2TIfb9qVJnu6bUIReO2D-QW68zThW-yngexF1S-vOKpkioS7mXVyty092kvKrS9rNj09SeaA5mZbfkU9OtduW3NsUp4aJXQWa6HMFohuCCVRFjB5hzHAdRCFyvP__TmZR5R_dI2jiqZ-Vk09DXFHb2AZPJYaZLM2IrvTrqsGdqSIUEk36pjfIMGxOWCRhPjnTq2H2yFeIuWxOiqru_98lh9_6fR99u8NqvZC4DFarEtO6M1q-cDML9Iz0dRFzGZZJR10DPzMCO193vufUpKBe6FRNIF0_cKemCcjXtPcds2eHV_EDHDkbN9PnhrEoYYHUgvnPh1s04jrKX-pA0gmhgnzo8hRkdfJfs0p0nqqHVlnpZyA1kkIYxZGXiR591y0igbb5ghMsXId5acDUkGBNbw5dGcJNFmEJczeIVy1nrDKul-bSrPGzvKogUzTmuXlk_wc8VsyFAeOc0DEKdSK2u8a2ietMO0UGg=&c=nfnzBYcMaIKfXjgzcdS5--O8kdCqui3nUY5lxKomvALPUms6SkiwqQ==&ch=oOpGZL43ZnytBynWs22-3n_Y0vtM81fHOIRKRoO7rBjqzNtcgnJzqQ==)".**

GBCAs are used with medical imaging devices called MRI scanners to examine the body for problems such as cancer, infections, or bleeding. GBCAs contain gadolinium, a heavy metal. These contrast agents are injected into a vein to improve visualization of internal organs, blood vessels, and tissues during an MRI, which helps health care professionals diagnose medical conditions. After being administered, GBCAs are mostly eliminated from the body through the kidneys. However, trace amounts of gadolinium may stay in the body long-term. Many GBCAs have been on the market for more than a decade.

There are two types of GBCAs based on their chemical structures: linear and macrocyclic (see Table 1 in Drug Safety Communication). Linear GBCAs result in more retention and retention for a longer time than macrocyclic GBCAs. Gadolinium levels remaining in the body are higher after administration of Omniscan (gadodiamide) or OptiMARK (gadoversetamide) than after Eovist (gadoxetate disodium), Magnevist (gadopentetate dimeglumine), or MultiHance (gadobenate dimeglumine). Gadolinium levels in the body are lowest after administration of Dotarem (gadoterate meglumine), Gadavist (gadobutrol), and ProHance (gadoteridol); the gadolinium levels are also similar across these agents.

**RECOMMENDATION**: Health care professionals should consider the retention characteristics of each agent when choosing a GBCA for patients who may be at higher risk for gadolinium retention (see Table 1 listing GBCAs). These patients include those requiring multiple lifetime doses, pregnant women, children, and patients with inflammatory conditions. Minimize repeated GBCA imaging studies when possible, particularly closely spaced MRI studies. However, do not avoid or defer necessary GBCA MRI scans.

Patients, parents, and caregivers should carefully read the new patient Medication Guide that will be given to you before receiving a GBCA. The Medication Guide explains the risks associated with GBCAs. Also tell your health care professional about all your medical conditions, including:

-   If you are pregnant or think you might be pregnant
-   The date of your last MRI with gadolinium and if you have had repeat scans with gadolinium
-   If you have kidney problems

Healthcare professionals and patients are encouraged to report adverse events or side effects related to the use of these products to the FDA's MedWatch Safety Information and Adverse Event Reporting Program:

-   Complete and submit the report Online: [www.fda.gov/MedWatch/report](http://r20.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001j_rjx58yXQeTBx9YFn-xnclTRGEaAqkrUuA52tNVqADHh8IZCT34A5MlhazHtwdSV_4PkjBhoGjF7YXACEYKm1KONo2ttbYNRHu2Guy-U4LgiYdJm7y5OFXzW6GFnVfR38MMdw9-8suKnIrCnIetV-_pXu_zP0CoD-IqDK-Y8vYmTGYdpThB7GhPHoGz0TLZ&c=nfnzBYcMaIKfXjgzcdS5--O8kdCqui3nUY5lxKomvALPUms6SkiwqQ==&ch=oOpGZL43ZnytBynWs22-3n_Y0vtM81fHOIRKRoO7rBjqzNtcgnJzqQ==)
-   [Download form](http://r20.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001j_rjx58yXQeTBx9YFn-xnclTRGEaAqkrUuA52tNVqADHh8IZCT34A8xX7DsmCEVEQYPkrZ0Q2pbEBVfiUFqTS9qksxfJj9g7vUleqi7dsGAUhV4r-_tn6wZDQFA1N_-304VRIcywv4q5OJkhEsuVxA2Tz5REsmd9a7Dlph56hWfznox9dd_IRTfw_hhngLc1D1GNY_VuxG5ZV7TBCBGGiOgTOIrXrmR84oC79KniVylUSP0_Og_WzZ7ItOKhAh9r8AChDK5DDMqW3A8p7A8VWEd4qkE9slN99X5inUHYO3NGw2qIesDGwpjUY9Ysscf868pWGMpY7JdwlU5uSyPTbODUcEaWu8xVSdrg0VNQu6ItFkwHcu2CaaK8yHIUqhcf3nlxupK6WOtqRcQUeA5CS345-BXzZLV05ChgCQyTkSaR5a-Dveojx0uTtzgaXif_b8dE7iWgh77lKnOL09thySzXXpLr5zeDCjO6ch6984hiDN-oths8BwUMNZSTfpvN&c=nfnzBYcMaIKfXjgzcdS5--O8kdCqui3nUY5lxKomvALPUms6SkiwqQ==&ch=oOpGZL43ZnytBynWs22-3n_Y0vtM81fHOIRKRoO7rBjqzNtcgnJzqQ==) or call 1-800-332-1088 to request a reporting form, then complete and return to the address on the pre-addressed form, or submit by fax to 1-800-FDA-0178

### Jack Kruse — 2018-06-12T03:54:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-contrast-warning.20742/post-243226>

Despite the FDA black box label in 2007 and warning in 2015, practitioners continue to order these scans routinely without informing patients of the risks. The black box warning is for nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF), a serious and sometimes fatal kidney condition, and anyone with kidney impairment is at risk. Gadolinium is normally excreted by the body through the kidneys, but kidneys functioning less than optimally have trouble getting rid of all of the gadolinium, so toxic levels build up in the body’s tissues and organs, including the brain.

The FDA warning that came in 2015 was the one gadolinium toxicity advocates were fighting for. It reported what they knew all along – even people with normal kidney function can accumulate gadolinium in their bones and vital organs.

**What You Need To Watch For**
Most symptoms of gadolinium toxicity mimic the chronic conditions many people get imaging for, but there’s one that sets it apart. People describe it as a feeling of being electrified just underneath the skin, and some people experience it as feeling like their skin is crawling. Other symptoms include joint pains, muscle twitches, and cognitive changes which usually start within the first month after the exposure. Also common are itchy skin, deep bone pain close to the site of the MRI, numbness or tingling, dry eyes, tinnitus (ringing in the ears), hair loss, balance problems and swelling in feet and hands.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-05T14:41:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mri-contrast-warning.20742/post-257291>

Researchers fine tune how Gadolinium causes its problems as it passes through the brain's water networks to cause the symptoms associated with this transition metal.
In a study published online on 25 November by Investigative Radiology, Dr. Katerina Deike-Hofmann and colleagues from the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ) at Heidelberg University Hospital and Essen University Hospital explain how gadolinium enters the cerebral spinal fluid (CSF). They show that GBCAs are excreted through specific entry points, such as the choroid plexus and the anterior chamber of the eye, into the CSF and subsequently enter the glymphatic system that, for instance, surrounds the perineural space. [https://www.auntminnieeurope.com/index.aspx](https://www.auntminnieeurope.com/index.aspx)

# Thread: Why will seaweed be more important in a 5G blue light world?  Deuterium fractions will rise.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-will-seaweed-be-more-important-in-a-5g-blue-light-world-deuterium-fractions-will-rise.20745/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-23T04:05:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-will-seaweed-be-more-important-in-a-5g-blue-light-world-deuterium-fractions-will-rise.20745/post-233222>

Why does the Epi-paleo Rx call for liberal use of seaweeds? Marine vegetation can mitigate ocean acidification, study finds. The reason however eludes them: PUFA's in marine vegetation have a lot of deuterium in them (Seafood/DHA does not) and this deuterium indirectly affects the salinity of seawater to affect the pH scale. Deuterium is capable of shifting pH because of its extra neutron compared to acids with H+. It turns out the oxygen isotope O18 is linearly related to deuterium content in water on Earth. The concentration of O18 in precipitation decreases with temperature. The coldest sites on Earth, in locations such as Antartica and Greenland, have about 5 percent less O18 and deuterium than ocean water. When this water melts into seawater it dramatically affect salinity of the sea. The same is true of CSF in your. When you liberate O18 and deuterium in your body it has a radical effect on your salinity. We see this in neurosurgial conditions like Diabetes Inspidus, SIADH, and ANF. Does this relationship in nature scale to nature? It does. Many of the most important records of past climate variability from the tropical oceans are based on proxies linked to the stable isotopic composition of seawater. In particular, carbonate records of 18O derived from fossil corals and marine microfossils and shells of seafood are key indicators of past changes in ocean temperature and salinity. What are the implications of this? As we live in a 5G blue light world we'll collect more deuterium and O18. As our tissues due it will decrease our intrinsic salinity and we'll need more salt not less as we irradiate ourselves with nnEMF to maintain our bone mass. [https://t.co/qaQ4xz72ak](https://t.co/qaQ4xz72ak)

I have low stomach acid, so I eat Betaine HCL tabs (which is stomach acid HCL)
but
it looks I may have to increase salt intake, for the chloride content.
That may also cause my kidneys to use less energy on reabsorbing salt,
my eGFR could be better.

Low salt intake = lower blood volume, higher pulse, higher heartbeat.

optimal amount 1 1/3 - 2 2/3 teaspoons a day
but 100grams= 6.7 teaspoons is ok, it was used before refrigeration days

To make it easier on me, I just put 2 teaspoons of Redmond Salt in the morning on a little dish and use it up over the day. Food eaten can be salted to taste separately (as additional salt).

Note, most kidney issues are decreased ability to reabsorb salt, so increasing salt intake is a positive event from kidney's pov.

time 17:00
kidney reabsorbs (recirculates) 3.5 pounds of salt daily.
it is about 60% of its metabolic energy (ATP use)
so I wonder why one or three teaspoons would make big difference?
That is a question to @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/)

Low sodium creates
low calcium and magnesium (taken from bones)----> bad bones, muscle cramps, etc
magnesium must bind to ATP (how many body weights of ATP we go thru the day?)
High aldosterone
osteoporosis
low collagen
kidney stones
low vit c ----> low cognitive function
low salt=low magnesium=low (magnesium-ATP)=low energy
high BUN
low salt-->over retaining water (swelling)
do not confuse (specially when working hard, sweating) thirst with low water, it is low salt (Gatorate have way to little salt)
sufficient salt allows body to sweat more, cool body, allowing harder work
low salt creates pregnancy problems
pregnancy is low blood volume state, sharing blood with fetus, need more salt
preeclampsia a condition in pregnancy characterized by high blood pressure, sometimes with fluid retention and proteinuria (can be solved by additional salt intake)
pregnant women need twice of the normal amount of salt
salt=basic foundational nutrient

caffeine (& tea) promotes flushing out Na & Cl (4 cups of coffee -->1 tsp of salt out)

**Foundational nutrients:**
**Sun, salt, Lugols, selenium**

Answer: young humans before full myelination in brain need deuterium to complete growth but once myelination is complete then mitochondria need to deuterium deplete to control growth and metabolism pathways into adult life. This is the control for the insulin signaling systems. **The proxy for the system is salinity measures.** As deuterium fractions in tissues rise as we age salinity measures lower. This is why those with insulin resistance have high BUN/creat ratios and why they are more apt to have SIADH, HTN. Na brings Vitamin C into the brain for TCA cycle protons recycling to lower the KIE of deuterium in TCA intermediates as we age to control volume changes in the matrix.. This is why SIADH is associated with cognitive decline in TBI and aging. Na also brings calcium into bone in cases of osteopenia. **Low Na = low Ca and Mg = mitochondrial dysfunction.** Low salinity in CSF and the body is a feature of all nnEMF exposure in environments. The higher nnEMF in your environment the lower salinity becomes in blod and CSF and lower salinity = sleep disorders. Most people with high insulin levels have sleep apnea due to deuterium rise. BUN rises as salinity decreases because deuterium fractions are increasing. Salt helps cool our body because it augments skin vasodilation ( like UVA light does). As man made blue light exposure of skin rises, dopamine creation in the eye and brain drop and our salinity in CSF drops and obesity manifests easier due to deuterium content in the choroid of the eye and the skin and subcutaneous fat mass due to melanopsin. The TRP receptors of melanopsin are affected negatively by deuteration by altered salinity. All makes sense Raymund when you understand bio-physics of the KIE of deuterium. Most don't. Most have no clue why PUFA's in fake fats are bad........they increase proton leakage in mito and allows more deuterium in matrix. ### Da-mo — 2018-01-27T16:49:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-will-seaweed-be-more-important-in-a-5g-blue-light-world-deuterium-fractions-will-rise.20745/post-233623>

Yes JanSZ, it counters hyp**ka**lemia. Sodium shortage will make hyperkalemia worse. This is because excess Ka is excreted in urine but if Na is low the animal will retain water to limit sodium loss.

Further than that - in cattle and horses at least, a lack of sodium chloride leads to a situation where the body substitutes with magnesium and robs it from bone and muscle to maintain levels in the blood. For this reason blood tests wont show Mg deficiency until things are really bad. In horses, it can take 2 years of Mg supplemantation to restore what was lost from the muscle.

Hi @[Da-mo](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9712/)

For long time I switched to Redmond Salt, but use it in "normal" amounts, read it as not enough.
I am getting muscle cramps at night.
On a metabolic profile (2013)
potassium=3.5 (3.4-5.4)
sodium =137 (134-146)
chloride = 102 (96 -108)
CO2 =24 (21 - 33)
magnesium on Spectracell looks good

For a while I was taking all three types of magnesium that @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) recommends in mitochondrial webinar.
That was not it. Cramps persisted.

When I switched to (Mg + Ka) aspartate (daily) my cramps went away (on the night that I took the pills).

Now, since Jack posted video on salt I place 2 teaspoons on a dish for consumption over the day and stopped (Mg+Ka) aspartate pills.
it is third day and so far no cramps.

That salt seems to help with cramps but have set me back with nightly trips to the bathroom.
After prostate surgery 8 years ago my bladder is very small and easily gets excited even with smaller content of urine and make me rush to the bathroom.

As of today I am 48 days on DDW.
Had a breakthrough on day 22, went to bathroom about 2 hrs after getting up.
That was a one time event only, but I measure what I pee.
Before DDW my usual max was about 250cc.
After that breakthrough I am holding more and more.
Last night was another record 325cc at 1AM followed by 350cc at 4AM, off the bed at 6AM.

This two items from Jack are really changing my life. DDW and salt.
Thank you Jack.
I am hoping that my balance of electrolytes will settle at some point and I will be able to sleep my 8 hrs without interruptions.

Hi all, having a hard time reconciling these suggestions with my own experience of eliminating salt entirely 7 months ago. I eat zero carb. I resolved muscle cramps that had persisted for years after stopping all supplements (magnesium etc) and cutting out salt. My natural thirst has also come down considerably.

While the leg cramps resolved I do have other issues I am working on. I am open to adding salt back in as an experiment to see if it changes anything now that I am practicing other foundational strategies recommmeded by Dr. Kruse. Just don’t want to mess with what I perceive as a balanced electrolyte system via the absence of cramps.

Is it possible to obtain enough salt from swimming in the ocean daily to negate the need for added dietary salt?

### kris90 — 2018-01-24T20:43:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-testosterone-low-free-testosterone.20747/post-233382>

Are you fasting, ketotic, doing CT and getting sun? If so, all of these things lower the release of sex hormones because they increase receptor binding affinity. That means less active (free) hormone is required to activate the androgen receptors.

Having high SHBG in this state is actually a good thing, because SHBG protects the androgen receptors from getting too much deuterium so they function better. It is a sign of superior metabolic flexibility and energy efficiency. That is why I have stopped chasing high T levels, because my lifestyle changes the biochemistry in my body to allow optimal receptor activity without the need of my testes producing massive amounts of hormone.

### kris90 — 2018-01-24T21:40:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-testosterone-low-free-testosterone.20747/post-233391>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=233388)
>
> I highly doubt that having high SHBG is a good thing.
>
> ...........

Context is key. That's why I said having high SHBG **in this state** (i.e. CT, keto, fasting) is a good thing.

Warm adapted mammals are designed to have higher T levels, because leptin is higher, while cold adapted mammals bind more of their T to SHBG.

[https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-017-03078-0#Bib1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-017-03078-0#Bib1)

> FT is considered the main component with hormonal activity, and serum SHBG is considered a transport protein for T to target tissues, regulating the circulating concentration of FT. **However, recent research has demonstrated that serum SHBG can directly affect cells by binding to their receptors and acting as a hormone**.

[https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/)

> It is well known that SHBG reduces cellular uptake of Te; however we have demonstrated that SHBG may also maintain stable levels of biofunctional Te and physiologically relevant intracellular Te levels by reducing Te glucuronidation and efflux. Another study has also demonstrated that LNCaP cells incubated in 30 nM DHT glucuronidated 85% of the DHT in 5 days. Upon addition of 140 nM SHBG, 95% of DHT was not taken up by cells and remained in the medium \[[23](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/#B23)\]. This rapid inactivation of Te by glucuronidation has been demonstrated in human clinical studies, where a single oral dose of Te did not alter serum total Te levels but rapidly and significantly increased levels of conjugated Te in serum \[[24](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/#B24)\]. However we acknowledge that the level of free Te in healthy men is much lower (174–729 pmol/L \[[25](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/#B25)\]) than that used in this study, since _in vivo_ SHBG is always present. **When SHBG is reduced or absent, excess free Te may enter cells and oversaturate the ARs since it has been reported that AR saturation occurs at approximately 2-3 nM in prostate \[**[**26**](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/#B26)**\].** The LNCaP cells may respond to this by rapidly glucuronidating and effluxing the excess Te. The steroid content of SHBG (Fitzgerald Industries International) was not specified; however it was purified from the serum of pregnant women which contains significantly lower Te than that of adult males \[[3](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/#B3), [27](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/#B27)\]. Even if all of the serum Te were carried over, the Te at the maximum SHBG concentrations used (125 nM) would be much less than 1 nM, significantly less than that used in our experiments. Importantly, SHBG treatment had no significant effect on gene expression when compared to untreated controls.

Sounds a lot like a feedback mechanism for preventing too much deuterium in the androgen receptors.

> To further explore the intracellular biological actions of SHBG the effects of SHBG on Te responsive genes were investigated. As expected Te upregulated PSA mRNA expression. However, despite the significantly lower intracellular Te in the presence of SHBG, addition of SHBG in combination with Te further increased the expression of PSA mRNA. Similarly, downregulation of AR mRNA expression by Te was also further enhanced when cells were incubated with SHBG and Te. Addition of SHBG also further decreased the Te downregulation of SHBG mRNA although, under these conditions, the reduction did not reach statistical significance. **In this experiment the cellular Te level was lower in the presence of SHBG than when Te was added alone; however the effects on gene expression were much greater. Moreover, maximal effects on gene expression were reached at a lower concentration of Te when SHBG was present.** Intracellular expression of SHBG in mouse proximal tubule cells has also been shown to prolong the expression of androgen responsive genes \[[10](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/#B10)\] and overexpression of SHBG in LNCaP cells has been shown to influence the expression of estrogen and androgen responsive genes \[[28](https://www.hindawi.com/journals/ije/2016/6437585/#B28)\]. **This would suggest that both endogenously expressed SHBG and internalised SHBG have similar positive stimulatory effects on androgen sensitive gene expression**.

SHBG seems to improve receptor sensitivity. Sounds like a good thing?

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I think T levels vary by latitude, and as T levels drop (at higher latitudes), SHBG rises, and vice-versa. It's a seasonal shift. But most people don't live a cold/ketotic lifestyle in winter due to modern conveniences and fake lights, so they continue to drive T levels up until they deplete the compound pharmacy.

Low T levels in a warm adapted state = problematic. In the cold/winter, it's likely not an issue SO LONG as you are adapted to that environment and respecting circadian rhythms.

### kris90 — 2018-01-26T17:02:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-testosterone-low-free-testosterone.20747/post-233555>

> [Antonis said:](/goto/post?id=233541)
>
> Mate @[kris90](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19157/) .I feel good to be honest and confident. No tired at all, just bored. Am horny during the day. My partner is aboard unfortunately.

Good. So your numbers to me are fine.

The coolest thing coming from myself, a guy who suffered secondary hypogonadism due to a leptin issue (chronic overtraining and underfeeding) is that once I became cold adapted, I went from hypogonad to godlike, and yet my T levels were basically unchanged from the time of my hypogonadism diagnosis. Check out bloodwork:

**Hypogonad (2014):**
LH: 2.0 IU/L (ref: 1-9)
FSH: 2.0 IU/L (ref: 1-19)
Estradiol: 37 pmol/L (ref: 40-160) LOW
Total Testosterone: 13.0 nmol/L (ref: 6.1-27.1)
Free Testosterone: 233 pmol/L (ref: 110-660)
Bioavailable Testosterone: 5.5 nmol/L (ref: 2.8-15.5)
SHBG: 40 nmol/L (ref: 13-89)

**Cold-adapted (2017):**
LH: 2.0 IU/L (ref: 2.0-9.0)
FSH: 2.0 IU/L (ref: 2.0-12.0)
Estradiol: <60 pmol/L (ref: 40-160)
Total Testosterone: 13.2 nmol/L (ref: 7.6-31.4)
Free Testosterone: 154 pmol/L (ref: 110-660)
Bioavailable Testosterone: 3.9 nmol/L (ref: 2.8-15.5)
SHBG: 72 nmol/L (ref: 13-89)

So the major difference in these 2 labs is SHBG levels, which proves my theory that SHBG is an important seasonal binding protein for androgens.

Yes, SHBG is a key player, and not always the bad guy. Receptor function is always more important than hormone levels, and in the cold, the magnetic flux is stronger, so binding of hormones to receptors are tighter. I believe it is a function of quantum electrodynamics.

In summer when you're eating a ton of carbs, you'll likely see your SHBG levels drop and Free T go up, because as mentioned, leptin rises with carb intake. This is a good thing in summer. The problem with conventional wisdom as Jack mentioned before is the biochemistry studies are done in a lab under blue light. So temperature and light are not controlled for appropriately which massively skews the results. Jack has laid out the biochemical changes between cold adapted and warm adapted mammals so beautifully in CT 6 blog.

>
> There will always be margin for lab error to consider as well. That is why your function, wellbeing and instincts are much better indicators of hormonal dysfunction than labs. Labs can help confirm suspicions.
>
> And keep in mind, hypogonadism (other than testicular failure/damage) always links back to leptin. Chronic stress/energy loss is what creates the cascade of hormonal imbalances all under the control of leptin. It is the master hormone used to account for electrons in the brain so the body/cell can make a choice based upon energy availability and energy requirements within a given environment.
>
> Maybe go and read The Quilt, if you have not done so yet:[https://www.jackkruse.com/the-quilthow-to-beat-agin/](https://www.jackkruse.com/the-quilthow-to-beat-agin/)

You know better. You had experienced this. I have read it. Also being non fat is good. Which ofcourse explains everything we always talk here

# Thread: Metabolic inborn error of beta oxidation of fatty acids
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolic-inborn-error-of-beta-oxidation-of-fatty-acids.20748/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eric Waldron — 2018-01-23T21:45:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolic-inborn-error-of-beta-oxidation-of-fatty-acids.20748/post-233272>

That's at least how Google translator translated it from Spanish to English. There's a young girl in my wife's hometown of Peru who was born with this disease. The mother is a friend of my sister-in-law. She was told that her daughter is unable to properly digest proteins, so is therefore only able to consume certain foods (not many) and supposed to drink a special milk that can be costly.

I researched it a little bit and see that, of course, it's a mitochondrial disease. So I'm sure JK's protocol would work wonders on her daughter who is three years old. Any suggestions? It's supposed to be an extremely rare disease/disorder.

Thanks much! And once again, I'm entirely grateful for Doc JK, you all, this site, and Tayta Inti (Meaning "Heavenly Father" in the ancient Peruvian language Quechua that they called their god, the SUN ).

Thanks again,
Eric

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-24T02:25:36+0200

### Eric Waldron — 2018-01-24T04:47:11+0200

metabolic inborn error of beta oxidation of fatty acids".

### Lahelada — 2018-01-24T05:42:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metabolic-inborn-error-of-beta-oxidation-of-fatty-acids.20748/post-233318>

I just had a bit of a look around. Under that name quite a few things come up. T. "Medium chain acyl CoA dehydrogenase deficiency seems to be the English term." However to be sure I would question with the docs as to whether this is what is meant by the term you quote.
I got my info from here [http://www.analesdepediatria.org/es...on-un-diagnostico-olvidado/articulo/13084185/](http://www.analesdepediatria.org/es/defectos-betaoxidacion-un-diagnostico-olvidado/articulo/13084185/)

# Thread: CT Frequency and Duration
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Greenie — 2018-01-24T01:48:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233300>

I have been doing CT on a daily basis for a couple of months now. Session lengths vary between 10-30+ minutes. As much as CT has been incredibly helpful, just about everything has diminished returns after a certain point. I have wondered if there is a point of redundancy after a certain amount of time or if I should be leaving a day or more in between sessions.

As much as ideal balance depends on the individual, has anyone experimented with CT regarding frequency and effectiveness? I am curious to hear what your experiences have been.

### Sandra — 2018-01-24T04:50:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233314>

A couple of years ago I was CTing about 3-4 times a week at 10-12 degrees C, for about an hour each time. I didn't notice anything 'magical' except for great sleep and some reduction in some minor shoulder pain/inflammation. I was hoping my cellulite would disappear and I would turn into a super model  But, I also didn't have a lot of weight to lose or any bad diseases or ailments, so I'm not sure what else I should have been expecting to see. I did feel good though

### Greenie — 2018-01-24T07:38:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233320>

> [Sandra said:](/goto/post?id=233314)
>
> A couple of years ago I was CTing about 3-4 times a week at 10-12 degrees C, for about an hour each time.

Thanks for the feedback, Sandra. Did you experiment with the duration at any point? If so, did you find an hour was more effective than less (or more) time?

### Sandra — 2018-01-24T12:37:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233327>

> Thanks for the feedback, Sandra. Did you experiment with the duration at any point? If so, did you find an hour was more effective than less (or more) time?

I didn't really experiment with different lengths of time. It took me 3-4 baths to work up to 1 hr, but then I just did an hour each time.

### Susie Cahill — 2018-01-29T12:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233725>

> [Sandra said:](/goto/post?id=233314)
>
> A couple of years ago I was CTing about 3-4 times a week at 10-12 degrees C, for about an hour each time. I didn't notice anything 'magical' except for great sleep and some reduction in some minor shoulder pain/inflammation. I was hoping my cellulite would disappear and I would turn into a super model  But, I also didn't have a lot of weight to lose or any bad diseases or ailments, so I'm not sure what else I should have been expecting to see. I did feel good though

Wow, that’s a cold temp and a reasonable amount of ct and a pretty respectable duration. Did you reduce any cellulite? I’m just faffing around with face dunks but I’ve had visions of a leaner me when I get the courage to fully submerge.

### shah78 — 2018-01-29T13:05:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233727>

Do more!! 2 -3 times a day, seven days a week. Shoot for 20-30 hrs a week. See what happens. good luck.

### drezy — 2018-01-29T15:55:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233733>

> [shah78 said:](/goto/post?id=233727)
>
> Do more!! 2 -3 times a day, seven days a week. Shoot for 20-30 hrs a week. See what happens. good luck.

Yeah once you get adapted it's worth some experimentation

> [Sandra said:](/goto/post?id=233314)
>
> I didn't notice anything 'magical' except for great sleep and some reduction in some minor shoulder pain/inflammation.... I did feel good though

Similar results for my wife:
weight loss and ease of control (after 3ish months) even though she only fluctuates 5 pounds from ideal at any given time
Lifelong insomnia turn into good sleep (morning sun plays a big role here too)
Moods got better and it's way easier to be married to her now

The timing of CT discovery was great too. Nearing our 15th anniversary it's nice to have a healthy vigorous partner in good moods in a solid marriage where we both (mostly) gave up trying to bend the other to our will and we're just chillin together now.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-29T16:00:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233735>

I think smaller times 5-10 minutes stretched out when your not eating is the best.

### Sandra — 2018-02-01T04:28:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-233892>

> [Susie Cahill said:](/goto/post?id=233725)
>
> Wow, that’s a cold temp and a reasonable amount of ct and a pretty respectable duration. Did you reduce any cellulite? I’m just faffing around with face dunks but I’ve had visions of a leaner me when I get the courage to fully submerge.

Didn't really notice any visual cellulite reduction. I didn't take measurements either so this is just anecdotal rather than specific unfortunately.

### Sajid Mahmood — 2018-02-03T20:09:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-234048>

When’s the best time to do CT to help or improve sleep? A hour before bedtime?

### Allin — 2018-02-04T01:58:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-234066>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=233735)
>
> I think smaller times 5-10 minutes stretched out when your not eating is the best.

_CT of short durations of 5-10 minutes when you are not eating is best, Reminds me of face dunking to turn on communication (dive reflex) from the nerves around your eyes to your gut and back. I usually drink about 500 ml cold water, then 40 minutes in cold pool (50-60 degrees) @9:00 am then an hour of sun after. Shorter duration multiple times brings what to the table? Longer duration brings what to the table? I need to give this idea more attention/thought. Am I doing a set of sprints/drag racing or running a mile/going 60 on the highway? I am going to mix it up but I am thinking I need to do more 40 minute sessions than the shorter ones._

### shah78 — 2018-02-04T02:36:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-234071>

Jack jarred my remory on this topic. For well over six months I only did twenty/twenty five minutes sessions. I would stay in the water till I started losing bodytemp. I'd do three or four a day somedays. eventually I got to thirty/forty minutes and eventually made it to over two hours. I forgot thee thermometer days.!
Good luck.

### Greenie — 2018-02-05T00:59:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-frequency-and-duration.20751/post-234143>

Thank you everyone for the feedback. Still experimenting with shorter durations (10-15 mins) done more frequently (daily) versus longer durations (30+mins) less frequently (2-3 times per week). One thing I have noticed is I feel as though a sort of internal heater turns on after about 12 minutes. At that time the feeling of the water, though unchanged in actual temperature, is warmer. Has anyone else experienced this sensation?

The notion that more of a good thing equals even better results, therefore more = better is a fallacy and an often dangerous one at that. I think most of us, if not all, have experienced this in one way or another, especially when we get excited by positive results. Over sharpening anything leads to its dulling. Knowing when to stop is at least as important in knowing when to start. Finding the balancing point is essential.

# Thread: Living with low dopamine husband who thinks Im crazy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### shannoncorrin — 2018-01-24T02:21:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233304>

I tried to search this because I'm sure its been discussed before, but didn't find what I was looking for. I have read the Dopamine Rx, I like when Dr. Kruse says " You remain silent when you do not care. You speak up when you do…even if you are a pain in the ass." I've tried to speak up to my husband, but he isn't willing to listen. I see his health slipping. He is a CPA, so that means he sits in front of a computer for 40-70 hours a week. He won't wear blue blockers (I'm his optometrist, and I tried to explain blue light with my "doctor hat" on but it didn’t work) , he wont even download flux. He is literally finds a reason to put down every suggestion I make. He will not give up grains and cheese ( I wish I could eliminate these from out house because they are keeping me off track). He makes fun of my grounding shoes. Rolls his eyes when I tell him not put toxic spray sunblock on. The list goes on… he isn't ready for help.

His dad past away a year ago due to complications of autoimmune disease. His death was the perfect example of how poor our medical system really is, and I have a major chip on my shoulder because of it. His lifestyle was never addressed, he was just put on medication after medication. My husband and his family thinks he died of pulmonary fibrosis. They don't seem to realize that if he was never put on the medication that caused the fibrosis, he may still be here.

I've listened to Dr. Kruse state that he had a similar issue convincing his wife to change. I think it was the transformation that he made that finally woke her up. I don't have any transformations to make. I'm fit and active. My only "disability" is dystonia that affect my gait when I walk and how I write (both of which I am self conscious about but don't think any thing will ever help).

I know I need to start small. Has anyone struggled with this? What did you do to improve the situation? How do you not sound like a "know it all". I almost think that if a different doc told him to wear blue blockers, he would be more inclined

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-24T02:33:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233306>

I'd ignore him. That is what I did wih my family until they slowly wake up. There is a chance he won't and if he does not then you have choices to make.

### brandie — 2018-01-24T03:48:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233311>

> [shannoncorrin said:](/goto/post?id=233304)
>
> I tried to search this because I'm sure its been discussed before, but didn't find what I was looking for. I have read the Dopamine Rx, I like when Dr. Kruse says " You remain silent when you do not care. You speak up when you do…even if you are a pain in the ass." I've tried to speak up to my husband, but he isn't willing to listen. I see his health slipping. He is a CPA, so that means he sits in front of a computer for 40-70 hours a week. He won't wear blue blockers (I'm his optometrist, and I tried to explain blue light with my "doctor hat" on but it didn’t work) , he wont even download flux. He is literally finds a reason to put down every suggestion I make. He will not give up grains and cheese ( I wish I could eliminate these from out house because they are keeping me off track). He makes fun of my grounding shoes. Rolls his eyes when I tell him not put toxic spray sunblock on. The list goes on… he isn't ready for help.
>
>
>
> His dad past away a year ago due to complications of autoimmune disease. His death was the perfect example of how poor our medical system really is, and I have a major chip on my shoulder because of it. His lifestyle was never addressed, he was just put on medication after medication. My husband and his family thinks he died of pulmonary fibrosis. They don't seem to realize that if he was never put on the medication that caused the fibrosis, he may still be here.
>
>
>
> I've listened to Dr. Kruse state that he had a similar issue convincing his wife to change. I think it was the transformation that he made that finally woke her up. I don't have any transformations to make. I'm fit and active. My only "disability" is dystonia that affect my gait when I walk and how I write (both of which I am self conscious about but don't think any thing will ever help).
>
>
>
> I know I need to start small. Has anyone struggled with this? What did you do to improve the situation? How do you not sound like a "know it all". I almost think that if a different doc told him to wear blue blockers, he would be more inclined

I've been there. I shared info on Facebook, no one cared, and the ones I cared about most didn't even engage me. All of whom knew I had been quite ill and improving. I actually got quite PISSED about it. I shared more, my words expressed how frustrated I was that no one cared, or heeded my warnings. Then...

I forgot all about them and focused solely on me. I made an assumption that nothing was incurable, even my incurable condition.

I decided that I can't live to help anyone else in any fashion, only me. If my husband teased me, I flipped him off. He finally realized I was changing and he had better accept my change. I didn't give an ultimatum, my energy did. And in this process (long, emotional process) I became better. My personality returned, is returning, and the me I had become (weak, quiet, indifferent) is almost a distant memory.

I'd never say "leave him". I'd say see if he likes the you that you become.

For me, I decided that life is too short to allow someone to control your thinking or being. I gave my power away to everyone, taking it back is painful, emotional but necessary. I didn't have the strength to do this before I improved.

Incidentally, he's almost fully on board, except moving away from friends and family and 20 years with the same company. He was even ok with oil lamps.

If this isn't a life threatening situation, give him time to see how you've benefited.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-24T15:26:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233344>

I'm more hardcore. If you are not good enough for you who are you good for. FOCUS on you always. His reaction will tell you all you need to know without any words. Always pay attention to actions in case like this. You learn to be resilient when you focus solely on you; this is how you survive when you know you are capable of making worthy contribution to the world sans the anchors driving you down; you only let it bother you or fight it when you have nothing to lose and so much to gain. When my wife realized I was not waiting on her, and she was being left behind by her own volition, things began to change..............but that change would not have happened unless I played hardball with her.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-24T15:29:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233346>

Some things cannot be fixed in life. They can only be carried. This is why your redox is tied to your resilence in life. If your redox drops the loads you are carrying around begin to affect you. Your past effects your present and future and you begin to spin your wheels. If you just get up every morning when your load is to heavy to carry and see the sunrise everyday, your redox will rise and you'll begin to see that life's adversities have the remarkable ability of introducing the real you to yourself. This is how the "what's in life, become the "why's in our life and allow us to walk into our passions.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-01-24T15:30:26+0200

### Billybats — 2018-01-24T16:28:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233363>

Do like the above said. My boyfriend made fun of me not so much in a bad way but thought this was another guru trip, but no. At first I talked about it now I just do what I have to do to help me and he has been pretty good but then he didn't care so I stopped talking to him about and just do what I have to. A few days ago we were making coffee as I passed by him staring out the window at the sunrise and smiled without a word , kept on walking. I actually got him to finally buy my all natural moccasins for my constant grounding during this cold weather. I thank God he is understanding what I tell him about this site.

### JanSz — 2018-01-24T16:59:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233368>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=233306)

> I'd ignore him. That is what I did wih my family until they slowly wake up. There is a chance he won't and if he does not then you have choices to make.

Good time to let off some steam.

You just told me to ignore those on this list:

pancreatic cancer, person just got home for hospice care ( I think that means less than 6 months prognosis)(high 70's male)
metastasized prostate cancer, 79yo
enlarged prostate, surgery in planning (80yo), coronary angioplasty, pending, doctor works on him as I write this
two female breast cancers (60 yo mom and her daughter, got them at the same time)
intestinal problems, IBS or Crohn's (male in high 50's)
Crohn's multiple surgeries (male in 40's)
meningioma now, twice angioplasy in the past ( female in 80's)
MS (male in 30's)
Parkinson's (65 yo male)
Autism (male 20 yo), his younger brother with spina bifida

Then there are the currently "healthy"

..

### brandie — 2018-01-24T20:50:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233383>

Your past effects your present and future and you begin to spin your wheels. Someone said on another post:
You need to move to feel better; you need to feel better to move
This IS dopamine in a nutshell!

### Michalis — 2018-01-25T05:52:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233423>

There is always the forum though so it is a good point to come back to and see how people are doing so even with low dopamine you get a little inspired to change things.

### Da-mo — 2018-01-25T10:11:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/living-with-low-dopamine-husband-who-thinks-im-crazy.20752/post-233432>
